title: This Prehistoric Giant Salamander Grew to Almost 4 Feet Long
gist: Kyoto University paleontologists reanalyzed 3.5-million-year-old fossil vertebrae from Japan's Oita prefecture, originally attributed to the same genus as the living Japanese giant salamander, and determined they belong to an entirely new extinct genus, published in the journal PeerJ.

themes.theme: A newly recognized extinct salamander genus

insights.claim: Fossilized vertebrae excavated in the 1990s from the Ajimu region of Japan's Oita prefecture, dated to 3.5 million years old, were originally thought to belong to the genus Arias (the same genus as the living Japanese giant salamander), but a new Kyoto University analysis found their unique structure made them an unlikely fit, and comparison against six living giant salamander species led researchers to classify them as an entirely new genus.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A concrete example of fossil reclassification driven by comparative morphological analysis against living relatives — illustrates how taxonomic revisions still occur even for well-studied fossil sites decades after excavation.
insights.locator: In the 1990s, paleontologists excavating in the Ajimu region

insights.claim: The living Japanese giant salamander grows up to 5 feet long and is among the largest amphibians alive today; the newly identified extinct relative reached almost 4 feet long.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Size comparison data point situating the new fossil genus relative to its still-living giant amphibian relative.
